impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder that conditions patent licenses on customers' exclusive purchase of unpatented products engages in unlawful tying under the Sherman Act, and whether the defense that the tied products are 'nonstaple goods' lacking substantial non-infringing uses shields the patent holder from antitrust liability.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the jury's finding that Ingevity engaged in unlawful tying by conditioning licenses to its fuel vapor canister patent on customers' exclusive purchase of Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products. The court upheld the antitrust damages award after the jury found the honeycombs had substantial non-infringing uses beyond the patent's scope. The patent had separately been invalidated under § 102(g).
impacts.positiveImplications: Competitors like BASF gain protection from exclusionary licensing practices by patent holders who leverage patents to foreclose competition in unpatented product markets.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders who tie unpatented product purchases to patent licenses face significant antitrust liability, including damages, even where the patent is later invalidated. Companies must ensure their licensing practices do not condition access on exclusive purchasing of unpatented goods.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether genuine disputes of material fact precluded summary judgment on inequitable conduct in patent prosecution and on a Walker Process fraud claim for attempted monopolization based on a fraudulently obtained patent.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgment on both inequitable conduct and Walker Process fraud, finding genuine disputes of material fact on both issues. Tenaris had acquired prior-art documents (the CYMAX Documents) but allegedly failed to disclose them during prosecution of its coiled tubing patents. The court held that whether Tenaris's nondisclosure constituted inequitable conduct and whether it rose to the level of Walker Process fraud required factual resolution at trial.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent challengers benefit from the court's willingness to allow Walker Process fraud and inequitable conduct claims to proceed to trial where there is evidence of nondisclosure of material prior art acquired through corporate acquisitions.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies acquiring assets that include prior art documents face heightened risk that failure to disclose such documents during patent prosecution could support both patent unenforceability and antitrust liability under Walker Process.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the International Trade Commission's practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential in antidumping and countervailing duty investigations is consistent with the governing statute and whether section 1516a(2)(b) abrogates the common law right of public access to judicial records.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the Court of International Trade's holding that the ITC's blanket confidentiality designations for questionnaire responses are incompatible with the statute and that 19 U.S.C. § 1516a does not abrogate the common law right of access to judicial proceedings. The court treated the ITC's mandamus petition as an appeal under the collateral order doctrine and ruled on the merits.
impacts.positiveImplications: Importers, domestic competitors, and the public gain greater transparency into trade remedy proceedings, enabling better-informed participation and accountability in antidumping and countervailing duty investigations.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies that submit commercially sensitive data in ITC questionnaire responses face increased risk of public disclosure, potentially exposing proprietary business information to competitors.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to a server that tailors content delivery specifications to the capabilities of a requesting wireless device are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that GoTV's patents claiming methods for server-side tailoring of media content to wireless device capabilities were ineligible for patenting under § 101, reversing the district court's judgment of infringement and entering judgment for Netflix. The court found the claims directed to the abstract idea of tailoring content to device capabilities, with no inventive concept transforming the claims into patent-eligible subject matter.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies like Netflix gain protection from enforcement of broadly-claimed patents directed to conventional server-side content adaptation for different devices, reducing patent troll litigation exposure.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders with claims directed to content delivery optimization face heightened § 101 invalidity risk, even after surviving district court challenges and obtaining favorable jury verdicts.

impacts.caseName: Coda Development s.r.o. v. Goodyear Tire & Rubber Company
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court properly granted judgment as a matter of law overturning a $64 million jury verdict for trade secret misappropriation of self-inflating tire technology, and whether correction of inventorship was properly denied.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the district court's grant of JMOL overturning a jury verdict of $2.8 million in compensatory and $61.2 million in punitive damages for trade secret misappropriation under Ohio law. The court also affirmed denial of correction of inventorship of Goodyear's patent. The decision turned on the sufficiency of evidence linking Goodyear's product development to Coda's disclosed trade secrets.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies that independently develop technology similar to disclosed trade secrets gain reassurance that JMOL can correct insufficiently supported jury verdicts, even large ones.
impacts.negativeImplications: Trade secret owners face a high evidentiary bar to sustain misappropriation verdicts; even jury findings of misappropriation with significant damages can be overturned if the causal link between disclosure and the accused product is not sufficiently established.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the Patent Trial and Appeal Board governing discretionary denial of inter partes review institution constitute a 'general statement of policy' exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking under APA § 553(b).
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the PTO Director's instructions guiding Board discretion on IPR institution decisions are general statements of policy exempt from APA notice-and-comment requirements. The court emphasized that the Director retains ultimate authority over non-institution decisions, that no party has a legal right to IPR institution, and that the instructions do not bind the Director.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the PTO's broad discretion to decline IPR institution without procedural constraints, reducing challenges to their issued patents.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies and patent challengers like Apple, Cisco, Google, and Intel lose their argument for greater procedural safeguards around IPR institution decisions, leaving the PTO Director with broad, largely unreviewable discretion to deny IPR petitions.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Board correctly construed claim terms in a patent related to streaming partly encrypted media content, specifically regarding the scope of 'encryption information' in the context of adaptive bitrate streaming systems.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the Board's claim construction in an IPR involving DivX's patent on streaming encrypted media content, vacated the Board's decision upholding the patent, and remanded. The court adopted a different construction of 'encryption information' that could render the claims obvious over prior art, potentially invalidating the patent.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming companies like Netflix benefit from the Federal Circuit's willingness to correct Board claim constructions that improperly narrow prior art and shield patents from invalidity challenges in the digital media space.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the streaming technology space face risk that claim constructions favorable at the Board level will be reversed on appeal, reopening obviousness challenges.

impacts.caseName: US Patent No. 7,679,637 LLC v. Google LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to web conferencing systems with time-shifting capabilities for asynchronous viewing of data streams are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the district court's dismissal of a patent infringement suit against Google for failure to state a claim, finding that the web conferencing patent claims were directed to patent-ineligible subject matter under § 101. The claims covered systems enabling participants to view web conference content asynchronously at different playback rates.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies gain continued protection from broadly-claimed web conferencing patents, reinforcing the § 101 defense as an effective tool for early dismissal of infringement suits.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders asserting claims in the web conferencing and streaming space face significant risk of early dismissal under § 101, even at the pleading stage.

impacts.caseName: Adnexus Inc. v. Meta Platforms, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court erred in dismissing a patent infringement complaint against Meta's Lead Ads product for failure to state a claim, where the plaintiff appended claim charts and infringement contentions to the amended complaint.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated the district court's dismissal of Adnexus's patent infringement suit against Meta and remanded for further proceedings. The court found that the amended complaint, supplemented by detailed claim charts and infringement contentions, sufficiently stated a claim for patent infringement against Meta's Lead Ads product.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the court's recognition that detailed claim charts and infringement contentions appended to complaints can satisfy pleading standards, making early dismissal more difficult for defendants.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies like Meta face reduced ability to obtain early dismissal of patent infringement suits at the pleading stage when plaintiffs include detailed infringement mappings.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether OPM's regulatory writing requirement for overtime authorization in 5 C.F.R. § 550.111(c) is a valid exercise of rulemaking authority under 5 U.S.C. § 5548(a), given the statutory requirement in 5 U.S.C. § 5542(a) that overtime be 'officially ordered or approved.'
impacts.description: In an en banc decision, the Federal Circuit upheld OPM's longstanding regulation requiring written authorization for federal employee overtime as a valid exercise of delegated rulemaking authority. The court affirmed the dismissal of an IHS nurse's claim that she was owed overtime pay for work performed during the COVID-19 pandemic without written preauthorization. A four-judge dissent argued the writing requirement impermissibly added a condition not found in the statute.
impacts.positiveImplications: Federal agencies benefit from the validation of the overtime writing requirement, which provides a clear administrative mechanism for controlling overtime costs and limiting back-pay liability for unwritten overtime work.
impacts.negativeImplications: Federal employees, particularly healthcare workers who performed extensive unwritten overtime during the COVID-19 pandemic, cannot recover overtime pay absent written authorization, even where supervisors were aware of and benefited from the extra work.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether IHS registered nurses hired under Title 5 but paid base rates under Title 38 are entitled to enhanced compensation for overtime, nighttime, Sunday, and holiday work under 38 U.S.C. § 7453 and related Title 5 regulations.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit panel reversed the Court of Federal Claims' dismissal of claims by IHS nurses seeking enhanced pay for overtime, nighttime, Sunday, and holiday work. The court held that the nurses adequately stated claims that IHS was required to apply Title 38 enhanced compensation provisions to their work, potentially entitling them to significant backpay. The case was remanded for further proceedings.
impacts.negativeImplications: Federal healthcare agencies like IHS face potential class-wide liability for failing to apply enhanced compensation provisions to nurses and other healthcare workers, with significant backpay exposure for overtime, nighttime, holiday, and Sunday work.

impacts.caseName: Secretary of Defense v. Pratt & Whitney
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Armed Services Board of Contract Appeals' decision on the inclusion of 'Drag' costs in the overhead pool under cost-plus government contracts was final and appealable, and whether the underlying agreement regarding Drag was valid.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit addressed cost allocation disputes in Pratt & Whitney's cost-plus contracts with the government, holding that the Board's decision on the overhead allocation base was not final (dismissing that portion) but that the Board's finding of an enforceable agreement regarding 'Drag' costs was final and appealable. The court reversed the Board's finding that the Drag agreement was enforceable, invalidating it and remanding for further proceedings.
impacts.positiveImplications: The government benefits from closer scrutiny of cost allocation methods used by defense contractors, particularly where unconventional 'collaboration agreements' with suppliers inflate indirect costs charged to government contracts.
impacts.negativeImplications: Defense contractors using collaboration agreements or unconventional cost-sharing arrangements with suppliers face risk that these structures will be challenged and invalidated under Cost Accounting Standards, potentially requiring significant cost reallocation and repayment.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding expert testimony on induced infringement of spinal surgery patents and in granting judgment as a matter of law to the defendant.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of two expert witnesses and JMOL in favor of DePuy in a patent infringement case involving spinal derotation surgical tools. The court found the district court abused its discretion in excluding experts whose testimony was consistent with the court's own claim construction, and remanded for a new trial.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the Federal Circuit's scrutiny of district court expert exclusion decisions, particularly where excluded testimony is consistent with the court's adopted claim construction.
impacts.negativeImplications: Medical device companies face renewed litigation exposure when expert exclusion and JMOL rulings are reversed, requiring new trials on infringement claims involving surgical techniques.

impacts.caseName: Micron Technology Inc. v. Longhorn IP LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Circuit has appellate jurisdiction over a bond order imposed under Idaho's Bad Faith Assertions of Patent Infringement Act, and whether the Act is preempted by federal patent law.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit dismissed appeals from an $8 million bond imposed on patent assertion entities under Idaho's Bad Faith Assertions of Patent Infringement Act for lack of jurisdiction, finding the bond order was not a final decision. The case raised significant questions about state-level regulation of patent enforcement, including whether such laws are preempted by federal patent law.
impacts.positiveImplications: Large technology companies like Micron operating in states with bad-faith patent assertion statutes benefit from state-level tools that impose bonding requirements and deter abusive patent enforcement by non-practicing entities.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent assertion entities face significant financial barriers when state bad-faith assertion laws require large bonds before litigation can proceed, potentially chilling legitimate patent enforcement. The jurisdictional dismissal leaves the preemption question unresolved.

impacts.caseName: Game Plan, Inc. v. Uninterrupted IP, LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether common law trademark rights acquired through an asset purchase agreement can establish priority for purposes of cancelling a registered mark under Section 2(d) of the Lanham Act, and whether the TTAB properly cancelled a registration where the registrant submitted no evidence at trial.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the TTAB's cancellation of Game Plan's 'I AM MORE THAN AN ATHLETE' trademark registration and dismissal of Game Plan's opposition to Uninterrupted IP's applications. The court held that UNIP properly acquired common law priority rights through an asset purchase agreement that transferred both the mark and associated goodwill, and that Game Plan's failure to submit any evidence at trial was fatal to its claims.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies acquiring trademark rights through asset purchases gain confidence that properly structured assignments transferring goodwill establish priority for cancellation proceedings, even when the assignment occurs after opposition proceedings begin.
impacts.negativeImplications: Trademark registrants who fail to actively present evidence in TTAB proceedings risk cancellation of their registrations, even where they hold federal registrations that would otherwise provide a presumption of validity.
